data.title: From Tornado Cash to THORChain: The Great Migration of Hacker Money Laundering Routes
data.description:
Written by: Shannon@Jinse Finance On April 18, 2026, a shocking heist involving $290 million in assets struck Kelp DAO, once again bringing the vulnerability of DeFi into the spotlight. Although the crisis was followed by an epic "collective rescue" in the days that followed: Arbitrum acted swiftly to freeze more than 30,000 ETH in the hacker's account, and the DeFi United alliance, composed of various ecosystem parties, launched a technical recovery plan on Aave and successfully raised enough funds to cover bad debts. However, amidst the optimism that "the losses have been covered," a chilling detail was overlooked by most. Monitoring data shows that despite the heavy regulatory and governance efforts, the hackers still managed to complete a large-scale fund transfer with remarkable composure and efficiency through THORChain. They directly exchanged the remaining 75,701 ETH (approximately $175 million) for native Bitcoin on the blockchain, thus completely escaping the monitoring scope of the Ethereum ecosystem. This is not an isolated incident. People suddenly realized that THORChain had unknowingly evolved into the "ultimate hub" in hackers' money laundering schemes. A review of the industry's blacklist over the past two years reveals a shocking list of successes: from the FTX vulnerability exploiter (losses of $124 million) to the globally shocking Bybit hacker (losses exceeding $1.2 billion), and then to the Balancer vulnerability exploiter (losses of $120 million), these top attackers all chose THORChain as their final escape route. When traditional centralized interception and Layer 2 freezing mechanisms prove ineffective against these "native cross-chains," we are forced to re-examine: how exactly did this cross-chain behemoth, touted as "not reliant on packaged assets and permissionless," rise to prominence? While bringing a liquidity revolution to the industry, has its extreme pursuit of decentralization objectively paved a fast track to freedom for hackers' funds? I. The Rise of THORChain In August 2022, the U.S. Treasury Department's OFAC added Tornado Cash to its sanctions list, identifying it as an "illicit financial instrument" that helped launder more than $7 billion, including hundreds of millions of dollars stolen by the North Korean Lazarus organization. The sanctions mean that any interaction between a U.S. citizen or entity and the smart contract address of Tornado Cash is illegal. After the sanctions were issued, the Lazarus organization quickly adjusted its strategy—stopping the use of Tornado Cash and instead relying on Sinbad.io (a Bitcoin-based mixer) for money laundering. However, in November 2023, Sinbad.io was shut down by US authorities. Losing its preferred tool, Lazarus briefly returned to Tornado Cash in early 2024—after all, this protocol runs on decentralized smart contracts and cannot be physically shut down. But the real turning point came in 2025: with the $1.4 billion Bybit cryptocurrency theft, the largest in history, THORChain surfaced on an unprecedented scale, becoming the core infrastructure for North Korean money laundering operations. Researchers traced approximately $1.2 billion—85% of the stolen funds—through this network. Therefore, a more accurate description would be: the hackers' money laundering route underwent an iterative evolution of "Tornado Cash → Sinbad.io (shutdown) → brief return to Tornado Cash → large-scale shift to THORChain," rather than a simple one-to-one replacement. The rise of THORChain is a natural consequence of regulatory crackdowns following the sanctions that cleaned up other tools, and also a result of THORChain's own architectural characteristics. Another detail worth adding is that on March 21, 2025, the U.S. Treasury Department officially lifted sanctions against Tornado Cash following a court ruling. The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that OFAC had no right to sanction Tornado Cash's immutable smart contracts because these contracts did not constitute "property" under IEEPA. This means that Tornado Cash has legally regained its freedom—but hackers have already established more efficient money laundering pipelines on THORChain.

II. Major Hacking Cases Using THORChain to Transfer Funds

Jinse Finance has compiled some hacking incidents involving the use of THORChain to transfer funds:

The seven cases span from 2023 to 2026, and a clear evolutionary trajectory can be seen from them:

In terms of tool selection, in early cases (Atomic Wallet, FTX), THORChain was just one of several tools, but in the Bybit case, it had become the absolute main force responsible for 85% of the laundering.

In the case of Bybit, THORChain was just one of several tools.

In the case of Bybit, it had become the main force responsible for 85% of the laundering.

In terms of tool selection, in early cases (Atomic Wallet, FTX), THORChain was just one of several tools, but in the case of Bybit, it had become the main force responsible for 85% of the laundering.

From the attackers' behavior, the three-stage strategy of "attack-dormant-resurrection" is becoming increasingly sophisticated. Balancer used a five-month dormancy period in both attacks, while the KelpDAO case saw money laundering begin almost immediately after the attack, a significant increase in speed. A noteworthy detail is that on April 24, 2026, the Balancer attacker's associated address was activated after the Kelp DAO attack, transferring stolen funds to THORChain again. To make matters worse, on April 24, THORChain announced on its official Twitter account that it would integrate native ZCash exchange in the coming weeks, and on April 27, it stated that Monero's implementation on THORChain was closer than ever before. This means that in the future, attackers could add an extra step of ETH/BTC→ZEC or XMR on top of the ETH→BTC step, using ZCash's transaction blocking feature or XMR's privacy features to completely sever on-chain traces, adding another barrier to an already difficult tracking effort. III. THORChain's Operating Principles: Why It's Naturally Suitable for Money Laundering To understand why hackers choose THORChain, we must first understand its technical architecture. Basic Positioning: Cross-Chain Uniswap THORChain is an independent layer blockchain built on the Cosmos SDK, using the CometBFT consensus engine and the GG20 threshold signature scheme (TSS), allowing users to exchange native assets across multiple blockchains without a centralized third party. Here, a key term needs to be emphasized: native assets. Unlike most cross-chain bridges that require packaging BTC into WBTC or ETH into various synthetic tokens, THORChain achieves true native asset exchange—you send real BTC and receive real ETH, without trusting any intermediaries or handling any packaged tokens. Core Mechanism: RUNE as the Universal Hub THORChain uses a Continuous Liquidity Pool (CLP) model, with its native token RUNE serving as the intermediate settlement asset for each exchange. Each liquidity pool within the protocol consists of an "asset + RUNE" pair, requiring a 1:1 value ratio between the two. When a user wants to exchange BTC for ETH, the system actually performs two steps: first, BTC is exchanged for RUNE in the BTC/RUNE pool, and then RUNE is exchanged for ETH in the ETH/RUNE pool. To the user, this appears to be a direct exchange, but at the underlying level, RUNE acts as a value hub between all assets.

Security Architecture: Threshold Signatures and Node Rotation

THORChain safeguards user assets through "Vaults" running on multiple chains. These vaults are protected by a threshold signature scheme, meaning no single node can completely control the funds. Node operators must stake a large amount of RUNE tokens as collateral, and malicious behavior is subject to slashing. Simultaneously, the protocol maintains a decentralized network structure by rotating nodes through a periodic "churning" mechanism.

Streaming Swaps: A Slicing Tool for Large Transactions

In July 2023, THORChain launched the "Streaming Swaps" feature, allowing users to split large transactions into a series of sub-swaps and execute them over a period of time to achieve better price execution and reduce price slippage.

This feature is an experience optimization for ordinary users, but it has a completely different meaning for money launderers—it provides a tool for automatically fragmenting large amounts of illicit funds, similar to manually distributing funds to thousands of addresses, but more efficient and covert. IV. Why Hackers Turn to THORChain: Six Structural Reasons Reason 1: No KYC, No Permissions, No Blacklist This is the most fundamental reason. THORChain's official slogan even directly states "censorship-resistant infrastructure." Its protocol design does not technically include any address blacklist function; any wallet address can be used indiscriminately. This is especially crucial compared to Tornado Cash: While Tornado Cash is also a decentralized protocol, its core function is "coin mixing"—achieving anonymity by pooling user funds and shuffling payment records. This model is naturally identified by regulatory agencies as "a tool specifically designed to hide the source of funds." THORChain was originally designed to solve legitimate cross-chain liquidity issues, and its regulatory focus is relatively low. Reason Two: Native Cross-Chain – The Most Effective Means to Cut Off the Tracking Chain Cross-chain bridges have replaced mixers as the preferred tool for money laundering for structural reasons: bridging can disrupt linear transaction tracking in a way that mixers simply cannot – allowing funds to “chain-hop” between incompatible ledger architectures. Most bridging protocols operate in the form of permissionless smart contracts and lack the design to detect or freeze illicit funds, allowing attackers to disperse large amounts of funds across dozens of chains before any alerts are triggered. THORChain is particularly powerful in this dimension: a sum of ETH can be converted into BTC through THORChain, and BTC can then be converted into the privacy coin XMR through other means. The entire tracking chain is broken the moment of the cross-chain transaction – because the transaction histories of the Bitcoin blockchain and the Ethereum blockchain are independent of each other, no analytical tool can natively track cross-chain fund flows, and can only rely on heuristics for inference. Reason 3: Sufficient Scale Prevents Large Funds from "Flooding" Liquidity. This is THORChain's unique advantage over many competitors. THORChain's liquidity pools have held billions of dollars in locked value at peak times, meaning that single exchanges of tens of millions of dollars will not produce abnormally large slippage and are less likely to be identified as "abnormally large transactions" by monitoring systems. In contrast, many smaller decentralized exchanges lack sufficient liquidity depth; once large amounts of illicit funds flow in, they will immediately cause price disturbances, leaving obvious on-chain traces. Reason 4: True Decentralization Makes Law Enforcement Difficult to Implement. When agencies such as the FBI requested THORChain to block wallet addresses related to the Bybit theft, THORChain's node operators refused—some node operators insisted that decentralized protocols should not choose who can or cannot use them. Critics point out that THORChain is not as decentralized as it claims. Many of THORChain's node operators are publicly identified and reside in jurisdictions with strict anti-money laundering regulations, such as the United States—meaning they maintained the network's operation knowing it handled hundreds of millions of dollars in stolen North Korean funds, with clear and traceable on-chain records. However, this incomplete decentralization creates a "gray area": ​​centralized enough for ease of use, decentralized enough to evade legal responsibility. Reason Five: Regulatory "Mole-Playing" Creates Demand The Lazarus organization's tool iteration trajectory is a living history of regulatory arbitrage: Tornado Cash sanctioned → shift to Sinbad.io → Sinbad.io shut down → brief return to Tornado Cash → large-scale shift to THORChain. Each regulatory action pushes demand towards the next available tool, and THORChain, as the largest and most liquid permissionless cross-chain protocol currently available, is precisely the most suitable choice. Reason Six: Tacit Economic Incentives for Node Operators A disturbing incentive structure exists here: THORChain node operators charge a fee for processing every exchange—including those stolen from Bybit. The hundreds of millions of dollars in transactions that flowed through THORChain from the Bybit hack generated millions of dollars in fees for node operators. Riccardo Spagni, former lead maintainer of Monero, aptly points out that US authorities investigated the developers of Tornado Cash partly because they gained direct financial benefits from Lazarus's use of Tornado Cash. THORChain node operators face the same issue. V. Industry Warnings and Future Trends Cross-chain bridges have replaced mixers as the primary money laundering infrastructure for hackers in recent years, with funds flowing through THORChain accounting for a large portion of this. Currently, there is no indication that THORChain or its node operators are under investigation in any jurisdiction for their role in the Bybit hack. But if the authorities responsible for enforcing sanctions decide to take action, they can trace the node operators—many of whom are publicly identified and reside in the United States. For THORChain, it stands at the same historical juncture that Tornado Cash once stood at: facing a fundamental question—does decentralized financial infrastructure have the responsibility and the capability to refuse to process funds from sanctioned entities? As THORChain community developer "TCB" warned on X: "When the vast majority of your transaction traffic is the proceeds from the largest financial theft in human history, this becomes a national security issue. This is no longer a game." Will history repeat itself? The developers of Tornado Cash have already provided an answer. THORChain's node operators may need to seriously consider their choices before it truly becomes a national security issue.

data.title: After eight years, Powell bids farewell, saying "See you next time."
data.description:
"I won't see you next time." With his term nearing its end, the Powell era is entering its final countdown. On April 29, 2026, at the end of a regular Federal Reserve press conference, before stepping down from the podium, Chairman Jerome Powell uttered these seemingly casual yet profound words to the reporters present: "Thank you very much, and goodbye until next time." He then left the podium and walked out of the hall, concluding his last press conference as Chairman of the Federal Reserve. On May 15, 2026, Powell's term as chairman will officially end. On that day, his successor, Kevin Warsh, Trump's nominee, is expected to be confirmed by the Senate Banking Committee and take over this most powerful monetary policy position in the world. Eight years, two terms, spanning two presidents, a once-in-a-century global pandemic, and the most severe inflation in the United States in forty years. Powell left behind a history of mixed achievements and controversies: Under his leadership, the Federal Reserve successfully maintained employment, keeping the average monthly unemployment rate at 4.6%, lower than his predecessors Greenspan, Bernanke, and Yellen; however, at the same time, the average inflation rate during his tenure was as high as 3.09%, far exceeding the Fed's 2% policy target and also significantly higher than the average of previous chairmen. When discussing his policy legacy, Powell quoted Frank Sinatra, saying, "I have some regrets, but not many." This statement is perhaps the most fitting footnote to his eight years in office. An Unconventional Central Bank Governor: From Princeton Liberal Arts Graduate to Fed Chair

In November 2017, then-President Trump announced the nomination of Jerome Powell to succeed Janet Yellen as the 16th Chairman of the Federal Reserve. This appointment immediately caused a huge uproar in academic circles.

The reason is simple: Powell is not an economist.

For the past thirty years, starting with Greenspan (1987), every chairman of the Federal Reserve has held a doctorate in economics and is a top scholar in the field of macroeconomics.

For the past thirty years, starting with Greenspan (1987), all chairmen of the Federal Reserve have held doctorates in economics and are top scholars in the field of macroeconomics.

Powell's resume stands out as particularly unusual on this elite path. He graduated from Princeton University in 1975 with a Bachelor of Arts degree and then went on to Georgetown University Law School, where he earned his Juris Doctor degree. His career began as an investment banker in New York, followed by a stint at the Treasury Department, where he served as an official in the George H.W. Bush administration, handling domestic financial, debt, and tax policy. From 1997 to 2005, he worked as a partner at the prominent Washington-based private equity firm Carlyle Group, before moving to the Global Environment Facility as a managing partner in 2008. In 2011, then-President Obama nominated him to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. This move was widely interpreted at the time as an olive branch extended to Republicans. In 2012, the Wall Street Journal revealed that Powell's personal assets were between $21.3 million and $72.2 million, making him the wealthiest member of the Federal Reserve Board at the time. However, his wealth accumulation on Wall Street could not compensate for his lack of academic background. Greg McBride, chief financial analyst at financial services firm Bankrate.com, bluntly stated, "Jerome Powell doesn't have a PhD in economics, but he will be entrusted with the responsibility of guiding the world's largest economy. Putting a non-economist at the helm of the Federal Reserve is a break with tradition." Critics were even more pointed. Josh Byvens, research director at the Economic Policy Institute, called for Yellen to remain, noting, "Now is the time for a true macroeconomics expert to take the helm, not someone who might be subservient to the board." Some commentators cited G. William Miller, the "last non-economist to serve as Federal Reserve Chair," as an example, pointing out that Miller was forced to resign after only 17 months in office during the Carter administration due to misjudging inflation, warning that history could repeat itself. However, Aaron Klein, an economics fellow at the Brookings Institution, holds a different view. He believes that Powell's years of practical experience at the Treasury Department and the Federal Reserve make him fully qualified for the position. More importantly, his unique background may precisely help the Federal Reserve break free from long-standing "groupthink." "There's no magic formula exclusive to PhDs in economics," Klein says. "Having a chairman with a different background can be an advantage—provided he knows when to set aside models and make judgments based on intuition." This debate will be tested in the most dramatic way over the next eight years. Taking Over in 2018: Inheriting a Start Full of Hidden Reefs On February 5, 2018, Powell was sworn in, officially taking over the reins of the Federal Reserve from Yellen. On the surface, he inherited a fairly good situation: inflation was below the Fed's 2% policy target, hovering around 1.5%; the unemployment rate was 4.1%, the lowest in 17 years; economic growth had regained momentum after years of stagnation, the stock market had repeatedly hit record highs, and the Trump administration's tax reform plan was injecting fiscal stimulus into the economy. However, hidden reefs have already quietly gathered beneath the surface. Veteran observer David Wessel pointed out at the time that Yellen left Powell with far more than just a brilliant report card; she also presented him with a series of thorny problems: How to manage the pace of interest rate hikes before inflationary pressures intensify? How to assess the impact of large-scale tax cuts on an economy already operating at near full capacity? When and how to use unconventional monetary policy tools to address the next recession? Other critics are even more pessimistic. They believe that Yellen's loose monetary policy has been maintained for too long, allowing US stock market valuations to climb to levels seen only three times in the past century, global government bond yields to fall to historic lows, and credit risk to be systematically underestimated—all of which will become time bombs for Powell. Even more troublesome are the political variables. Just five months into Powell's term, Trump publicly attacked him on CNBC: "I don't like that we're putting so much effort into the economy and then just watching interest rates rise." Powell chose to ignore this. But this political struggle with Trump, which has lasted for years, has only just begun. In the fall of 2018, Powell stated in an interview that the Federal Reserve "had a long way to go" to reach a neutral interest rate level, immediately triggering significant market volatility. That December, his statement that balance sheet reduction was "on autopilot" sparked another wave of market panic. Trump even considered firing him. These two controversies taught Powell a profound lesson: as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, every word he uttered had the power to shake the market. The Impact of the Pandemic: "Those Who Crossed the Line" in the Crisis If the initial policy adjustment period after taking over was merely a warm-up, then the spring of 2020 was Powell's first real test. In early 2020, the COVID-19 pandemic swept the globe, and the US economy plummeted to the brink within weeks. In April 2020, the US unemployment rate soared to 14.8%, the highest since modern unemployment statistics began in 1948, with tens of millions of Americans losing their jobs overnight. Faced with this once-in-a-century crisis, Powell's swift and aggressive response astonished Wall Street. The Federal Reserve swiftly lowered its benchmark interest rate to near zero, while simultaneously restarting and significantly expanding quantitative easing, purchasing trillions of dollars in bonds within weeks. At the same time, Powell, in conjunction with the Treasury Department, launched a series of emergency credit tools far exceeding the boundaries of traditional central bank operations. He later admitted that these actions far exceeded the scope of conventional monetary policy. "We crossed many red lines," Powell said at a Princeton University event in May 2020. "In situations like this, you do it first, and then figure out how to fix it later." This gamble ultimately paid off. The U.S. economy successfully avoided a second Great Depression, and the job market recovered largely from the devastation of the pandemic in about two years—a process that took six years after the 2008 financial crisis. Powell thus won widespread praise and was seen as a leader who demonstrated Volcker-esque decisiveness in the face of crisis. However, the phrase "figure out how to fix it later" also sowed the seeds for subsequent policy mistakes. The Era of High Inflation: From "Temporary" Arrogance to "Volcker-Style" Iron Fist The costs of pandemic-driven economic stimulus began to materialize in 2021. With massive fiscal stimulus funds flooding the market, consumer demand rebounded explosively, but the recovery of global supply chains was far slower than expected. Simultaneously, the labor market remained tight, and costs such as energy, rent, and wages rose in turn. This dual imbalance between demand and supply, coupled with the subsequent surge in energy prices triggered by the Russia-Ukraine war, pushed US inflation to the brink of spiraling out of control. In August 2021, facing the reality of significantly escalating inflationary pressures, Powell made a judgment at the annual Jackson Hole central bank symposium that he would later deeply regret—he called the current inflation "transitory," believing that supply chain disruptions would eventually subside and prices would return to normal. This judgment became his biggest policy mistake during his tenure. As it turned out, inflation not only failed to subside "transitorily" on its own, but instead continued to accelerate. As of February 2022, the US core CPI had climbed to 6.4% year-on-year, the highest level in forty years; in June of the same year, the composite CPI further surged to a peak of 9.1%. This was not only a misjudgment by the Federal Reserve, but also an indelible stain on Powell's historical reputation. During the congressional hearing, senators directly questioned: Why did the Federal Reserve make such a serious misjudgment of the inflation situation? Powell acknowledged that this wave of inflation suddenly and rapidly climbed after mid-2021, exceeding the expectations of almost all mainstream macroeconomists; the recovery of supply chains was also much slower than expected, a situation almost unprecedented in history. He admitted, "We should have acted sooner." The belated correction was unprecedented in its intensity. In March 2022, the Federal Reserve officially began this round of interest rate hikes. In the following year and a half, Powell's aggressive and continuous rate hikes shocked the market—in less than two years, the benchmark interest rate was cumulatively raised by more than 500 basis points from near zero, a pace of increase rarely seen in the modern history of the Federal Reserve. In stark contrast to the crisis bailout in 2020, this time, Powell's inspirational figure has shifted from Keynes to Volcker. At the 2022 Jackson Hole Economic Symposium, he explicitly warned that bringing price stability "back" would inflict "some pain" on jobs and growth, and declared that he would "do whatever it takes to maintain price stability," thus conveying to the market his determination to combat inflation with an iron fist. Critics pointed out that this was a belated shift, for which the Federal Reserve paid a heavy price—millions of American families suffered a significant reduction in purchasing power during this period. However, some economists defended Powell, arguing that the main cause of this wave of inflation was supply-side disruptions brought about by the pandemic and geopolitical shocks, and not a result that monetary policy alone could control. The most surprising outcome was that this unprecedentedly aggressive interest rate hike did not trigger the widely anticipated economic recession. By the end of 2024, the US economy maintained an annual growth rate of 2.5%, inflation fell sharply without pushing up unemployment, and the labor market remained close to full employment. The recession predicted by almost the entire economics community never materialized. Powell himself admitted in a Harvard economics class that the almost impossible "soft landing" was one of his proudest achievements. A Legacy and Controversy: Independence is the Greatest Legacy. History's ultimate assessment of Powell may not lie in any specific economic indicator, but rather in a more fundamental question: He upheld the independence of the Federal Reserve. Trump, during his first term as president, frequently criticized Powell for refusing to lower interest rates and even considered firing him. After Trump's return to the White House in 2025, this political pressure escalated into a direct confrontation—the White House instructed the Justice Department to launch a criminal investigation into Powell for cost overruns in the renovation of the Federal Reserve headquarters building, a move almost unprecedented in the Fed's 112-year history. Analysts generally believe that the real motive behind this investigation is to force the Fed to lower interest rates to align with Trump's political agenda. Faced with this unprecedented political pressure, Powell released a video statement in January 2026 to publicly retaliate, characterizing it as "the consequence of the Fed setting interest rates based on judgments in the best interests of the public, rather than conforming to the president's preferences." This video quickly circulated widely in financial circles, winning him bipartisan congressional support and allowing him to make a historic exit at the end of his term. In the history of the Federal Reserve, the last time a chairman faced such intense political pressure was more than fifty years ago: Nixon pressured then-Chairman Arthur Burns to maintain loose monetary policy, ultimately leading to runaway inflation. In contrast, Powell's resilience and refusal to compromise elevate him significantly above his compliant predecessor in history. Full employment is another commendable legacy of Powell. During Powell's tenure, the average monthly unemployment rate was 4.6%, lower than Greenspan (5.5%), Bernanke (7.3%), and Yellen (5.1%). Behind these figures lies tangible improvement in people's lives: the low unemployment rate asymmetrically benefits the most vulnerable groups in the labor market—between 2019 and 2024, the real wages of the bottom 10% of workers increased by a cumulative 15.3%; the unemployment rate for Black people fell to 4.8% in 2023, a record low. Researcher Dean Baker writes, "Powell's earnest implementation of the full employment goal has enabled millions of workers to retain their jobs even when they could have been unemployed, and tens of millions to receive wage increases they wouldn't otherwise have received." Powell's critics also have their reasons. On the regulatory front, the sudden collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in 2023 exposed the Federal Reserve's laxity in bank supervision. Baker bluntly stated, "He had serious deficiencies in regulation, which led to a situation where Silicon Valley Bank had to be bailed out." Regarding inflation, Powell's average inflation rate during his tenure was 3.09%, more than one percentage point higher than the Fed's 2% target, and also higher than the periods of Greenspan (2.5%), Bernanke (1.84%), and Yellen (1.17%). At the time of his departure, the Fed's balance sheet was still approximately $6.7 trillion, more than double what it was when he took office. His successor, Kevin Warsh, has made this "legacy burden" a top priority for rectification. Low unemployment and high inflation constitute the two sides of his two-sided report card to history. He himself defined the essence of this job in one sentence: "We are building dikes, not preventing hurricanes." This is both a humble statement about the boundaries of the Federal Reserve's functions and his self-interpretation of these eight years—not that he foresaw everything, nor that he never made mistakes, but that in the most turbulent times, he tried to build the institution strong enough to prevent it from being completely destroyed by the storms of politics, the torrential rains of the pandemic, and the floods of inflation.

data.title: What's Next After the Bubble: A 2026 Digital Asset Market Analysis Report
data.description:
Foreword: The Certainty and Uncertainty of Crypto

At the beginning of 2026, coinciding with a new bull-bear market transition, the entire market was filled with anxiety. After 1011, market liquidity began to dry up. In the following period, apart from a few leading projects and companies that remained, many more teams chose to close down or transform.

After the emergence of Openclaw, a new wave of technological advancements further amplified the panic with immense uncertainty. As market liquidity shrank, countless Crypto workers turned to AI. Media organizations that originally focused solely on Crypto suddenly had AI-related reports on their front pages, and even OGs who had been involved in the field for over a decade were proclaiming "Crypto is dead."

With the bursting of the crypto bubble, is Crypto really dead?

If you throw this question at AI, they'll give you countless answers. DeepSeek will tell you that the crypto market's boom has ended, and it's now the domain of professional, compliant players; ordinary people have no chance. If you ask Grok, it will tell you it's just a shift from bull to bear in crypto, weeding out some but also pushing crypto in a better direction. If you consult Gemini, it will say that AI development will drive crypto's development in tandem. There's too much noise, so we want to find the answer to this question in our own way. There's nothing new under the sun; we vaguely remember the market saying the same thing when the dot-com bubble burst in 2001, and it's been said for every bubble. So this time, we've chosen to study bubbles. Even if the answer is wrong, it's our own certainty.

I. Exploring the Historical Cycle: From Railways to the Internet, How Tech Bubbles Cycle Historically

Railway Glory and Radio: The Bubble-Ups and Downs of the Industrial Revolution

On September 27, 1825, the world's first railway, built in Britain, officially opened. Three years earlier, despite opposition from feudal nobles and religious figures, capitalists saw the future value of this steel behemoth and chose to bet on it, ultimately building it. They believed this technology would bring them profits, but they did not realize the impact it would have on the entire era.

Although the first railway was merely a branch line built as part of the canal transport system, its convenience and cost-effectiveness led to a rapid expansion of the entire industry, attracting numerous investors. Towards the end of the South American mining speculation bubble of 1824-1825, these venture capitalists began investing in railway companies. In 1836-1837, with the overall stock market boom, railway company share prices doubled. The British Parliament saw an opportunity and approved 44 more companies that year, whose total financing easily exceeded the industry's total previous capital. Like countless bubbles in later years, when a new technology is gradually accepted by the market, it rapidly develops, creates a bubble, and then quickly bursts. Then, as infrastructure is gradually improved, a new bubble emerges, stronger than ever before, eventually returning to normalcy. After the establishment of these 44 companies, due to the lack of a comprehensive railway network, railway transportation was not as convenient as traditional water transport, and the railway stock index began to decline during this period. However, by the early 1840s, valuations began to rebound and approached their previous peaks. Before 1843, the average annual investment in railway companies was approximately £1 million (equivalent to about $3.5 billion today). In 1844, this figure rose to £20 million (20x), and in 1845 it approached £60 million (60x). By 1846, this figure had reached £132 million (equivalent to $120 billion today), and in the same year, the total length of newly built railways reached a record 4,538 miles. Everything seemed to be thriving. The Bursting of the Bubble and the Return to Value Undeniably, early railways were successful business projects, but due to investor optimism, stock prices quickly far exceeded the highest level that railway stocks could achieve under rational valuation. The first railways did have a first-mover advantage, but without entry barriers, this advantage would disappear. Ample market capital coupled with low technological/market barriers presented an excellent opportunity for subsequent competitors, leading to a continuous compression of profit margins for existing companies and ultimately creating an environment of continuously declining returns across the entire industry, commonly known as "involution." For market investors at this time, the first sign of the impending end of the boom was the disappearance of the huge premium on newly issued shares; only companies considered of high quality could maintain their stock prices. For the surviving railway companies, expanding and acquiring prime locations was undoubtedly the best option to maintain their valuation and competitive advantage, and leveraging bank loans could accelerate this advantage. Worse still, being in an emerging industry, most railway companies subconsciously underestimated the difficulty of railway construction, leading to actual costs far exceeding the initial valuations in their prospectuses. Over time, these companies' stocks became a complete financial game: railway dividends no longer came from corporate profits, but from capital injections and bank loans. Under this vicious cycle, bank interest rates were constantly raised, and after a certain critical point, railway companies could no longer sustain this capital cycle; the technological allure of capital suddenly vanished. Overnight, countless investors went bankrupt, and public praise for railway companies turned into condemnation. Faced with this situation, the British government was forced to pass a parliamentary act allowing for consolidation of the railway industry and abandoning nearly 20% of approved new railway projects. As surviving companies regained profitability, a wave of mergers and acquisitions began. Afterward, the glory of British railways was no longer a dazzling spectacle, but rather like the gentle, slow morning sunlight illuminating the land. Although the frenzied capital bubbles were unlikely to reappear, they undeniably nurtured the growth of the Industrial Revolution. Ultimately, the same story unfolded later in the Americas. Marconi and Radio As a footnote to the development of the era, the story of the railway came to a temporary close. With the continuous development of transportation, the distance between worlds was gradually shrinking. People could travel to distant places using these vehicles, or achieve the effect of transmitting information without leaving home through wired telephones and telegrams. Of course, the limits of information transmission speed should not be limited to this. In 1865, after Scottish physicist John Maxwell systematically proposed the theory of electromagnetic waves, several inventors began experimenting with various radio waves. Finally, in 1895, fortune smiled upon the Italian inventor Guglielmo Marconi. After successfully making a receiver shake a bell at a distance of 10 yards using his self-developed signal transmitter, he believed this distance could be increased. Marconi astutely recognized the future commercial value of this technology, applied for a patent in 1896, and began marketing his technology to government agencies. Soon after, he founded the Wireless Telegraph and Signal Company to develop and sell this wireless telegraph device. In exchange for relinquishing the patent, Marconi received £15,000 (equivalent to $6 million today) in cash and £60,000 (equivalent to $28 million today) in shares, relieving him of financial worries. That year, Marconi was only 22 years old. From War to Market As a rising star, Marconi quickly attracted attention from all sectors of society. In the early days of his company, Marconi recognized the global communication needs of the British Navy and in 1899 provided wireless equipment sales and consulting services to both the British and Italian navies. His first order was worth £6,000 (equivalent to $2.5 million today), and subsequent annual revenue exceeded £3,000 (equivalent to $1.25 million today). Despite securing national-level cooperation and support, the market remained skeptical about the technology's commercial viability. After several years of trial and error, Marconi adjusted his business model, shifting his sales strategy from direct sales to leasing. The biggest difference between this approach and traditional methods was the development of an ecosystem. Through such collaborations, he allowed any product or business to use radio equipment by paying only a partial rental fee, with the sole restriction that all customers could only communicate with Marconi's other customers. This strategy led to the emergence of countless radio stations and similar competitors. The rise of radio-related stocks: With the entry of Marconi and other technological competitors, the entire radio industry began to flourish, attracting a flood of capital. In Marconi's early days, although the company's financial reports showed losses, this did not dampen investors' enthusiasm: the technology and business model were still in their early stages, and losses were not unacceptable. Later, Marconi changed its name to RCA, and the technological advantages and business network it had built in the United States began to pay off. They combined the patents owned by AT&T, GE, RCA, and Westinghouse Electric, forming an impregnable business fortress, which led to explosive growth in RCA's sales revenue and profits. As the saying goes, "When one person attains enlightenment, even their chickens and dogs ascend to heaven," and upstream and downstream companies associated with RCA also enjoyed this wave of technological dividends. At the height of the market frenzy, some people could easily raise funds and list their stocks simply by registering a company related to "radio." The story that followed mirrored the earlier railway boom: countless capital and companies poured in, but as the dividends began to dissipate, bank loans started to be distributed as dividends, ultimately leading to market collapse and the disappearance of the dividends. However, unlike the railway boom, the commercial value of radio technology was so epoch-making that this technological prosperity lasted for nearly two decades. Once the radio infrastructure was completed, the potential for growth—from radios and broadcasting stations to televisions and radio media—was so vast that it could sustain the market at a high level of prosperity for a long time. Ultimately, the Great Depression arrived, and the capitalist game could no longer continue. People had to seek more difficult but also more practical means to increase the company's and product's real sales revenue and net profit. The Internet Wave at its Peak: A New Round of Technological Social Experiments After IBM's attempt at personal computers, and with Apple's further development, the penetration rate of computers in the mass market reached a new height. This also meant that some technologies that previously only existed in research laboratories began to emerge—the Internet. From the Ivory Tower to the Commercial Arena The origin and birth of the Internet have become well-worn topics, and will not be elaborated upon here. Compared to its birth, the path the internet took to commercialization is clearly more worthy of our study. A decisive factor in this transformation was the National Science Foundation's (NSF) decision to relinquish control of the National Research and Educational Research Network (NREN) and transition to privatization and self-profit operation. During this process, numerous key elements emerged, making the internet's widespread application possible: Apple's PC provided the hardware foundation, the World Wide Web provided the framework, and Mosaic provided the entry point. Coupled with NREN's commercial transformation, a giant industry began its magnificent journey. In the early days of commercial open source, not everyone saw this opportunity; many related companies chose a conservative approach. On the one hand, their knowledge and insights failed to make them realize the potential opportunities inherent in the internet; on the other hand, in the business environment at the time, industry giants primarily generated revenue through rapid expansion and building their own ecosystems, and they were naturally resistant to this extremely open new environment. Nevertheless, this wasn't necessarily a bad thing for the industry's development: the resistance of the giants provided ample market space and opportunities for new entrants. Netscape: The First to Take the Plunge As one of the earliest companies to take the plunge, Netscape's peak truly shook the entire market. In late 1994, Mosaic Communications was embroiled in legal disputes due to its name being identical to Mosaic's, ultimately leading it to change its name to Netscape Communications Cooperation. Although the company still had $12 million in cash on hand, the monthly cash outflow of $1 million forced Netscape to consider a business model transformation. Through a series of maneuvers, it changed its previous service model, rapidly capturing a large market share by offering a 30-day free trial followed by a $49 service fee, coupled with the overwhelming performance advantage of its products. Netscape's original intention was simply to improve its market valuation through market share, but this tactic proved too effective. In its August 1995 IPO, Netscape raised $140 million, propelling it to its peak. However, this very success also led to its downfall. The success of this sales strategy made Netscape complacent, and while basking in the joy of the IPO, they failed to consider how to build their competitive advantage. They neither strengthened their upstream and downstream moats through acquisitions, nor improved their products, nor even bothered with business partnerships with competitors, choosing instead the most foolish option: inaction. Ultimately, its fate was clear—once the market discovered this huge opportunity and Netscape, as a pioneer, had proven its worth, a flood of competitors entered the fray, and Netscape was eventually acquired by AOL. The story of Netscape is poignant, but broadly speaking, it was a significant event for market development. Countless profit-seekers and innovators joined this adventure, giving rise to a dazzling array of projects. Almost in the same year as Netscape's success, Jerry Yang and David Filo spent considerable time researching browser requirements and ultimately completed a highly efficient information indexing system, which they named "Yahoo." Meanwhile, at Stanford University, Sergey Brin and Larry Page explored information search engines, working on how to find desired information more quickly on the internet. When these ideas crossed the ocean, Jack Ma was inspired and began preparing for the development of "China Yellow Pages." The Ultimate Concept Bubble: Compared to past railway and radio technologies, the entry barrier for internet technology is significantly lower. It doesn't require hiring workers to build railways or lay cables, nor does it require government approval. As long as you understand the relevant internet knowledge, you can do whatever you want. The enormous wealth effect combined with the low entry barrier ignited a frenzy in the capital markets. At the very beginning of the bubble, the capital markets maintained a degree of caution. However, when they saw that rudimentary products like Yahoo and Google, born in garages, could rake in huge profits through advanced business models, they realized that their previous market valuation logic seemed to be failing. Coupled with the rapid rise in the prices of various internet technology stocks, investors had long since forgotten their previous doubts. Ultimately, for fundamental investors, the valuation of the TMT industry had been blatantly and indiscriminately exaggerated, and almost everyone believed there was no problem with it. As corporate valuations become increasingly audacious, professional analytical standards also begin to distort. Generally, the higher the stock price, the more likely analysts, based on the profit and loss statement, are to arrive at a higher valuation. To ensure the rationality of the valuation, when the original profit anchor can no longer support the current price, the valuation benchmark gradually shifts from profitability to revenue, and then further breaks down revenue into concepts such as "click-through rate" and "retention rate," using these to analyze a company's market prospects for the next few years. The whole logic is sound, but its most fatal flaw lies in how to ensure the effectiveness of business model analysis without the reference of past cases. The only prerequisite is to listen to the founding team's analysis, i.e., "storytelling." Ultimately, people stopped paying for the practicality of technology and started paying for the story—whoever had the most convincing and promising business story was more likely to raise more funding. A true FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) began. Initially, people would seriously design their businesses, but as the market became increasingly volatile, some discovered that even if their companies had nothing to do with the internet, simply registering a website allowed them to be categorized as TMT (Technology, Media, and Telecommunications) and enjoy market benefits. Undeniably, some extremely forward-thinking projects emerged in this storytelling frenzy, such as online shopping, online food delivery, and even online pet care. However, the problem is that without adequate infrastructure, a story is just a story. Ultimately, the same outcome played out again. For those companies in the stock market, few truly adapted to the times and survived. Most maintained this false prosperity through bank borrowing until bank interest rates reached a critical point, at which point the market collapsed. Data-Driven Bubble Indicators: How Internet Valuation Failed The story of history has been told simply, but to discover more valuable information, we need to transform these narratives into quantifiable and comparable macroeconomic and financial indicators, and find patterns within them. This section uses the dot-com bubble (1995–2002) as its core sample, supplemented by historical data from before and after the Great Depression of 1929 as a reference, systematically presenting the evolution path of macroeconomic data during the bubble's life cycle from four dimensions—valuation indicators, monetary environment, capital flows, and the real economy. These regular trends will provide a solid "invariant" benchmark for the cyclical analysis of the Crypto market in subsequent chapters. The extreme inflation of the price-to-earnings ratio (P/E ratio) is most directly reflected in valuation metrics. In each round of technology bubbles, the market's optimistic expectations for new technologies gradually push up valuation multiples until they deviate from any reasonable fundamental basis. This process is a gradual "anchor drift," where investors gradually accept increasingly outrageous valuation levels until the entire valuation system collectively fails. During the dot-com bubble, the NASDAQ Composite Index's P/E ratio soared to approximately 200 times at its peak in March 2000, far exceeding the peak level of 60-80 times for the Nikkei 225 Index during Japan's asset bubble. This figure means that investors were willing to pay $200 for every $1 of current earnings—in other words, even if company earnings did not grow at all, it would take investors 200 years to recoup their costs. More notably, over half of the NASDAQ-listed technology companies were operating at a loss at the peak of the bubble, making it impossible to calculate a meaningful P/E ratio. Meanwhile, the S&P 500's P/E ratio was roughly between 29 and 33 in 1999–2000, with readings above 45 appearing more frequently around 2002—while its long-term historical average was only around 15–20. The NASDAQ index still had a P/E ratio as high as 175 in March 2001, indicating that even though the bubble had begun to burst, the market's valuation correction was far from complete. Shiller CAPE Ratio: A Valuation Warning Across the Centuries

The Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings Ratio (CAPE, also known as Shiller P/E or P/E 10), developed by Nobel laureate economist Robert Shiller, smooths out short-term fluctuations by using the average earnings adjusted for inflation over the past 10 years. It is widely considered one of the most reliable indicators for measuring long-term market valuations. In over 140 years of historical data from 1881 to the present, the median CAPE of the S&P 500 is 16.04, and the average is approximately 17.17.

At three landmark bubble moments, CAPE significantly exceeded the "dangerous threshold" of 30 times: reaching 32.56 times on the eve of the Great Depression in 1929, followed by an 89% market crash, with the index not fully recovering until 1954; and reaching a record high of 44.20 times during the dot-com bubble in 2000, after which the S&P 500 fell 49% between 2000 and 2002, and the NASDAQ fell 78%, with investors achieving only about -1.4% annualized real return over the decade from 2000 to 2010. Historical data shows that when CAPE exceeds 30 times, the average annualized real return over the following decade is only 0-3%, far below the long-term average of about 7%. It is worth noting that CAPE is not a "market timing tool"—a high CAPE cannot predict when a crash will occur, but it is extremely effective in predicting a period of low returns over the next decade. As the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis pointed out in its research report, although the impact of the bursting of the tech bubble in 2000 on the real economy was relatively mild, the destruction of wealth for stock market investors was profound. The extreme divergence in price-to-sales ratio (P/S) Due to the large number of listed companies being in a loss-making state at the peak of the bubble (more than half of the NASDAQ-listed technology companies had no positive earnings in March 2000), the P/E ratio has effectively lost its reference value. Therefore, the price-to-sales ratio (P/S) has become a more reliable indicator of the severity of a bubble. Research from the CFA Institute shows that in March 2000, the median P/S ratio for "Internet Content" companies reached 32.44, while by September 2020, the median P/S ratio for the same category was only 3.15—a difference of more than 10 times. The median price-to-book ratio (P/B) for the semiconductor sector also declined from 13.85 in 2000 to 3.32 in 2020.

The Double-Edged Sword of Monetary Policy: Easing Creates Bubbles, Tightening Bursts Them

Behind almost every large-scale asset bubble, the shadow of loose monetary policy can be found. Interest rate levels determine the "opportunity cost" of capital. When the risk-free rate of return is extremely low, funds naturally flow to high-risk, high-return assets, providing the most fertile ground for speculative behavior. When the central bank turns to tightening, raising interest rates and increasing the cost of capital, the fragility of the bubble is exposed.

Easing Cycles: Catalysts for Bubbles. The monetary policy background of the dot-com bubble began in the mid-1990s.

Easing Cycles: Catalysts for Bubbles. The monetary policy background of the dot-com bubble began in the mid-1990s.

Between 1995 and 1998, under Alan Greenspan's leadership, the Federal Reserve maintained a relatively loose interest rate environment, with the federal funds rate hovering around 5.25%–5.5%. More crucially, in the fall of 1998, the collapse of the Long-Term Capital Management (LTCM) hedge fund triggered systemic risk concerns, leading the Fed to cut interest rates three times consecutively, lowering the rate from 5.5% to 4.75%. Goldman Sachs, in reviewing this period, explicitly pointed out that the rate cuts following the LTCM incident "released a massive amount of liquidity," directly propelling NASDAQ from 11% of NYSE trading volume in 1990 to 80% of total stock market capitalization in 1999. This round of "insurance rate cuts" in 1998 greatly boosted investor confidence—TheGlobe.com's stock price soared over 600% on its IPO first day in November of that year, setting a Wall Street record. Tightening Cycle: The End of the Bubble. Starting in June 1999, the Federal Reserve recognized the risks of overheated asset prices and began a series of interest rate hikes. Within 10 months, the Fed raised rates six times, increasing the federal funds rate from approximately 4.75% to 6.5% in May 2000—an extremely high level since January 1991—and the discount rate to 6% (one of the highest levels since August 1991). This series of tightening measures significantly increased borrowing costs, making fixed-income products such as bonds more attractive relative to high-risk technology stocks, and funds began to withdraw from speculative assets. It is important to emphasize that interest rate changes did not burst the bubble in isolation; rather, they were a key variable in a series of catalysts. On March 13, 2000, Japan announced a renewed recession, triggering a global sell-off. On March 20, Barron's published a "Burning Up" cover story warning that internet companies were running out of cash. That same month, MicroStrategy was forced to restate its revenue due to aggressive accounting practices (its stock price plummeted 62% in a single day). Rising interest rates, external shocks, and a collapse in confidence—these three factors combined to form the complete trigger chain for the bursting of the bubble. Following the bursting of the bubble, the Federal Reserve also quickly shifted its stance. Throughout 2001, the Fed cut interest rates as many as 11 times, lowering the federal funds rate from 6.5% to 1.75%, one of the fastest easing cycles in its history. However, the aggressive rate cuts failed to prevent the continued deterioration of the job market—the unemployment rate only peaked at 6.3% in June 2003, three years after the bubble burst. This time lag in the transmission of monetary policy to the real economy is a crucial factor that cannot be ignored when understanding the consequences of the bubble. Capital Flood and Leverage Accumulation: From VC Frenzy to Retail Leverage If valuation metrics are the "thermometer" of a bubble, and monetary policy is the "fire source," then venture capital (VC), the IPO market, and margin debt are the "fuel" that continuously accumulates within the bubble. One of the core characteristics of a bubble's expansion phase is the influx of capital into speculative assets at an increasingly rapid pace and with increasingly lower barriers to entry—from professional venture capital firms to investment banks' IPO underwriting, and then to leveraged trading by ordinary retail investors, forming a complete speculative chain. Venture Capital: From Careful Selection to Spending. The scale of venture capital during the dot-com bubble experienced explosive growth. According to NVCA data, annual VC investment in the United States soared from approximately $8 billion in 1995 to a peak of approximately $105 billion in 2000 (at current prices), a 13-fold increase in five years. By 1999, 39% of US venture capital (VC) investment had flowed to internet companies. The direct consequence of this capital frenzy was a sharp decline in project quality—many startups lacking clear profit paths received huge sums of money simply because they had a domain name with a ".com" suffix. After the bubble burst, VC funds receded like a tide. VC investment in 2001 plummeted to approximately $36.5 billion—still the third highest in history, but a reduction of over 67% from the 2000 peak. Research by the CFA Institute further indicates that the average internal rate of return (IRR) for VC funds in 1999 ultimately recorded -4.29%, and even lower at -2.51% in 2000—meaning that institutional funds that entered the market at the height of the bubble's frenzy generally ended up losing money. IPO Market: From Feast to Freeze. The number of IPOs is one of the most sensitive indicators of market speculative sentiment. The number of US IPOs peaked at 677 in 1996, followed by brief adjustments of 474 in 1997 and 283 in 1998, before rebounding to 476 in 1999. In 2000, at the tail end of the bubble, 380 companies still went public. However, after the bubble burst in 2001, this number plummeted to only 80—less than a quarter of the previous year. During 1999, over 280 VC-backed companies completed IPOs, many of which saw their share prices rise by more than 100% on their first day of trading. VA Linux's 698% surge on its IPO day, December 9, 1999, remains one of the most extreme IPO first-day performances in the US market. Margin Debt: Leverage Peak. Margin debt is a core indicator for measuring market leverage levels and speculative sentiment. In the late 1990s, margin debt surged dramatically as retail investors flooded the stock market, peaking in March 2000—in sync with the NASDAQ index and venture capital investment—at approximately $300 billion (equivalent to about $500 billion in present value in 2000 dollars). The margin debt-to-nominal GDP ratio reached 2.6% during the dot-com bubble, a level that was again approached before the 2007 subprime mortgage crisis (2.5%), and significantly surpassed in 2021 (3.97%). During the 2000 market crash, individual investors not only failed to withdraw in time but actually accelerated their entry. Data shows that retail investors net invested approximately $260 billion in the stock market in 2000, higher than the $150 billion in 1998 and $176 billion in 1999. By the end of 2002, approximately 100 million individual investors had cumulatively lost about $5 trillion in market value. Vanguard research shows that by the end of 2002, 70% of 401(k) retirement accounts had lost at least 20%. This data reveals a typical pattern of retail investor behavior during a bubble—while institutions and insiders have already cashed out in large quantities, retail investors often become the last ones left holding the bag. The lagged transmission of the real economy: GDP contraction, employment collapse, and jobless recovery. The impact of asset bubble bursts on the real economy is often not immediate, but rather spreads gradually along a transmission chain from the financial markets to corporate investment and then to the labor market. While the US economic recession following the dot-com bubble was mild in terms of GDP, it left deep and lasting scars on employment and corporate investment, becoming a typical case of what economists call a "jobless recovery." GDP: The Superficial Recession and the Deep-Seated Wound. The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) defined the period from March to November 2001 as a recession, lasting approximately eight months. In terms of GDP, the recession was indeed relatively mild—real GDP contracted by only about 0.3% cumulatively, with an annualized decline of about 1.3% in the third quarter of 2001. However, the GDP figures masked a deeper structural wound: fixed investment (excluding inventories) declined continuously from 2001 until it bottomed out in the third quarter of 2002. Between 1996 and 2000, real business fixed investment in the United States grew at an average annual rate of about 10%, but this investment boom reversed dramatically after the bubble burst—the decline in business investment far exceeded the historical average. Job Market: From Historic Lows to Continued Deterioration. The story told by employment data is far more severe than that told by GDP. The US unemployment rate climbed steadily from 4.0% (a 30-year low) in September-October 2000 to a peak of 6.3% in June 2003—a year and a half after the NBER declared the recession over (November 2001). This unusual pattern of "recession over but unemployment still worsening" is key to understanding the aftermath of the tech bubble. The US Department of Labor estimated a net loss of approximately 1.735 million jobs in 2001 and another net loss of approximately 508,000 in 2002. By the end of 2002, the number of unemployed in the US had increased from approximately 5.6 million at the end of 2000 to approximately 8.4 million, an increase of 2.8 million, of which 1.7 million were long-term unemployed (more than six months). The technology sector was hit hardest. It is estimated that from 2001 to early 2004, Silicon Valley alone lost approximately 200,000 jobs. Manufacturing output fell by 7.6% from its peak in September 2000 to the end of 2001, far exceeding the 5.1% decline during the 1990–91 recession. This structural job loss differed from previous recessions—where lost jobs typically recovered in about three months and usually surpassed previous peaks in about 10–15 months—while the full recovery of employment after the 2001 recession continued into 2004, making it one of the longest job recovery cycles since World War II. Summary: A Four-Stage Evolution Model of Macroeconomic Data on Bubbles Based on the data analysis of the above four dimensions, we can summarize a four-stage evolution model of technology bubbles from their inception to their bursting from the perspective of macroeconomic indicators. This model has been repeatedly validated in the railway bubble, the Great Depression of 1929, and the dot-com bubble of 2000: • Stage 1: Mild Valuation Deviation (Emerging Stage). New technologies emerge, and early adopters and professional capital take the lead in positioning themselves. Valuation multiples begin to exceed historical averages (e.g., CAPE exceeds 20 times), but have not yet deviated from the explainable range. The interest rate environment is loose, the scale of VC grows steadily, and market sentiment is optimistic but rational. • Stage 2: Accelerated Valuation Climb (Frenzy Stage). Loose monetary policy combined with a technical narrative creates a positive feedback loop. CAPE exceeds the "dangerous threshold" of 30x, and P/E and P/S ratios reach extreme values ​​in certain sectors (e.g., NASDAQ P/E > 100x). VC investment grows exponentially (annual growth rate exceeding 100%), and the number of IPOs and first-day gains both reach record highs. Retail investors enter the market en masse, and margin debt soars in tandem. Market participants begin to use new frameworks to rationalize extreme valuations. • Third Stage: Valuation Collapse and Liquidity Depletion (Collapse Period). Central bank interest rate hikes or external shocks trigger a collapse in confidence. Asset prices plummet by 50-80% within months. VC funding dries up (annual decline exceeding 60%), the IPO market freezes, and margin calls trigger a cascading effect of forced liquidations. Assets with the most extreme valuations in the bubble (unprofitable companies, concept-driven projects) are the first to go to zero. • Fourth Stage: Transmission to the Real Economy and Prolonged Recovery (Adjustment Period). GDP contracted moderately, but job losses were severe and prolonged, far exceeding the recession itself (the unemployment rate peaked 2-3 years later). Business investment contracted sharply, and the market shifted from speculative growth to a focus on profitability and cash flow. Regulatory frameworks tightened ex-post (e.g., the Sarbanes-Oxley Act). Surviving companies (like Amazon) underwent years of revaluation, ultimately becoming the cornerstone of a new round of growth. A full recovery of the index could take 5-15 years or even longer. The core insight of this model is that, despite the different variables in each bubble (technology vehicles, market structure, participant composition), the underlying patterns revealed by macroeconomic data exhibit a striking consistency: This is precisely the "invariant" basis we can rely on when mapping this model to the Crypto market in later chapters. II. Crypto's Multiple Bull and Bear Markets: Economic Trend Cycles Unique to Blockchain BTC's Independent Evolution: From Cryptographic Experiment to Institutional Risk Asset Most past bubbles are now history, while we are currently experiencing new ones. If you have experienced multiple bull and bear market cycles in Crypto, you may have a similar understanding of these bubble patterns. Therefore, in this chapter, we will use BTC as the core benchmark and BTC and the overall Crypto market as samples to systematically analyze the similar yet unique lifecycle evolution paths of Crypto. These paths reflect the common patterns of human speculative behavior, yet exhibit significant differences due to the decentralized, global, instantaneous, and token-based economic characteristics of blockchain. After the bubble bursts, some tracks survive to form the basis of new cycles, others are eliminated, and still others undergo transformation—this is the core characteristic of the Crypto cycle. On May 22, 2010, a programmer named Laszlo Hanyecz posted on the Bitcoin Talk forum, offering 10,000 Bitcoins for two Papa John's pizzas. The deal was eventually struck, making him the first person in history to purchase real-world goods with Bitcoin. At the time, 10,000 BTC were worth only about $41, but today they are worth over $1 billion. This event is permanently commemorated as "Bitcoin Pizza Day," vividly documenting the astonishing transformation of BTC from a nearly worthless technological experiment to a core asset regarded by global institutions. Data shows that the peak market capitalization growth rate of each cycle has gradually converged from approximately 88 times in 2013, while the crash magnitude has decreased from 93% to approximately 47-48% in the 2024-2025 cycle (data as of March 2026). Behind this trend is the continued separation of the BTC and Altcoin cycles: BTC dominance is expected to remain stable at 58.6% in 2025-2026, and the US Spot BTC ETF has seen cumulative net inflows exceeding $55 billion since its approval in 2024 (with significant contributions from products like BlackRock IBIT). This figure not only far exceeds any single crypto product in history but also directly reflects that institutions now view BTC as an independent allocation target, rather than an adjunct to Altcoins. In contrast, the overall Crypto market still maintains a highly speculative financial attribute: in the early stages of a bull market, the density of new narratives is high, and there are many structural opportunities; in the later stages, a large number of homogeneous projects emerge, and liquidity is significantly diluted. The vast majority of altcoin projects quickly die or slowly disappear after the bull market ends. The main reasons include a lack of real users and products, rapid disproving of their narratives, and a persistent liquidity crunch after a sharp drop in token prices. This pattern was most clearly demonstrated in the 2022 Terra-Luna incident: LUNA's market capitalization once reached $40 billion, but its core narrative of an "algorithmic stablecoin" was instantly disproven after it de-pegged to UST, and its market capitalization plummeted to zero within days. On-chain data showed that its TVL (TVL) collapsed from a peak of $18 billion to less than $10 million, after which liquidity remained scarce and no real users recovered. According to DefiLlama's historical records, between 2021 and 2025, over 70% of DeFi and Meme projects experienced a TVL drop of over 90%, with most projects entering a slow death phase after the price collapse—trading volume remained low, developer activity stagnated, and they were ultimately completely forgotten by the market. From zero value to $13.6 trillion, a simple overview of BTC's evolution provides a more intuitive understanding of how its consensus value has developed: Unique Bubble Mechanism: Decentralized Speculation, Token Economics, and Network Effects. The formation mechanism of the Crypto bubble is essentially the same as that of the dot-com bubble, but their specific manifestations differ. The latter was mainly driven by venture capital, while the former was amplified through decentralized speculation, token economics, and network effects. The 2017 ICO boom is a case in point, raising approximately $5.3 billion throughout the year. Many projects raised funds based solely on white papers, resulting in a failure rate as high as 46-59%. During the period of macro-level monetary easing from 2020 to 2022, the supply of stablecoins rapidly increased from approximately $5 billion to over $150 billion (stabilizing at approximately $310 billion by 2026), further fueling leverage and speculation. Once external liquidity recedes, the problems of token economies lacking self-sustaining mechanisms become apparent: high-inflation designs and short-term incentive-driven growth essentially rely on continuous external funding injections. The core process of this round of the altcoin market can be explained using Everett Rogers's diffusion of innovation theory (S-curve). A bull market is essentially a process of speculative narratives spreading from early adopters to society at large: initially driven by innovators such as programmers, crypto VCs, and tech enthusiasts who enter the market based on their belief in technology; subsequently, KOLs, traders, and Web2 practitioners, as early adopters, amplify the narrative into a phenomenon through social media; after entering the mass market, ordinary office workers, students, and small business owners are attracted by the wealth effect and enter in large numbers; finally, the laggards—those with lower internet skills, relying mainly on short video recommendations, and prone to leverage—become the last wave of participants. When adoption approaches the 80-90% inflection point, new participants decrease significantly, marginal buying dries up, and the bull market ends. This process was particularly evident in the 2024 Solana Meme coin season, with over 5,000 new tokens issued in a single month, causing a severe diversion of attention and funds within the same sector, with most projects seeing their trading volume drop to zero within months. When the narrative spreads to the lower echelons of society, and early investors begin to realize profits, the upward price structure collapses, and the bull market comes to an end. From the perspective of market behavior, the fact that the population that can be mobilized by speculative narratives in society has been basically absorbed represents the beginning of the end of prosperity.

Cycles and Cycles: A Comparative Analysis of the Dot-com Bubble and the Crypto Bubble

During the dot-com bubble, the NASDAQ Composite Index's price-to-earnings ratio peaked at approximately 200 times in March 2000, with many tech companies unable to even calculate a meaningful P/E ratio (over 50% were operating at a loss); the median price-to-sales ratio (P/S) in the "Internet content" sector reached a high of 32.44 times. In contrast, the valuation bubble in cryptocurrencies was more directly reflected through the TVL/market capitalization ratio and fully diluted valuation (FDV):

2021 DeFi TVL peaked at approximately $180 billion, while the total Crypto market capitalization approached $3 trillion during the same period. Some memes and emerging projects saw FDV/TVL ratios exceeding 100. Between 2021 and 2025, over 70% of Altcoin projects maintained high FDVs despite significant TVL shrinkage, leading to a collective failure of the valuation system, far exceeding the degree of differentiation in burn rate during the internet era. If we want to analyze the reasons for this difference, from the structure of market participants to macroeconomic factors, we might find some clues. Fundamental Difference in Participant Structure: Grassroots Victory The internet bubble was dominated by institutional VCs and investment banks, with retail investors mainly participating indirectly through the stock market; capital led the market. Conversely, thanks to its decentralized underlying philosophy, Crypto is more directly dominated by global retail investors. The number of new addresses surged by tens of millions during the 2021 bull market, with participants rapidly spreading from tech geeks to the grassroots of society. This structural difference directly amplifies volatility: retail investors have a higher leverage ratio (perpetual contract holdings consistently exceed 60%), causing the impact of black swan events to far exceed the institutional buffers of the internet era. A typical example is the FTX crash in 2022, with total market leverage liquidation exceeding $20 billion, and the altcoin sector generally experiencing a monthly drop of over 60%. The devastating impact of macroeconomic tightening policies: The Fed's tightening cycle is the common terminator of both bubbles, but its impact on cryptocurrencies was more rapid and thorough. In the internet age, the Federal Reserve's six interest rate hikes in 1999-2000 (raising the federal funds rate from 4.75% to 6.5%) triggered a 78% collapse of NASDAQ. Crypto, on the other hand, faced the most aggressive tightening in history in 2022: the federal funds rate surged from 0% to 5.25-5.50% (a cumulative 525bp), and QT was launched during the same period, passively withdrawing approximately $2.4 trillion in liquidity (shrinking its balance sheet from $8.9 trillion to $6.5 trillion). Interest rate hikes directly increased the opportunity cost of speculative assets, while balance sheet shrinkage continuously drained on-chain liquidity pools. Altcoins, with their high beta, high leverage, and complete reliance on liquidity, were hit hardest, with their total market capitalization falling from a peak of approximately $2.5 trillion to less than $400 billion, far exceeding the decline in BTC during the same period.

The Chain Reaction of Black Swan Events and Liquidity Diversion

Crypto's unique global immediacy and cross-chain characteristics amplify both black swan events and internal liquidity diversion. During the dot-com bubble, external shocks (such as the MicroStrategy restatement in 2000) mainly affected a single sector; Crypto, however, triggered a chain reaction across the entire chain after the FTX incident: a deleveraging liquidation waterfall + the rapid listing of projects with homogenized narratives (Solana issued over 5,000 new tokens in a single month in 2024), resulting in severe dilution of funds in the same sector, leading to a collapse in trading volume and a slow death.

... Data from the 2022-2023 bear market shows that Altcoin trading volume shrank by more than 85% from its peak, and developer activity (GitHub commits) remained sluggish, further confirming the systemic transmission of liquidity depletion. The Evolution of Liquidity in the Crypto Bubble: The exponential growth in token issuance in the crypto market directly diluted overall liquidity, making it difficult for speculative bubbles to sustain. According to Bobby Ong, co-founder of CoinGecko, the platform currently tracks 209 chains, 1450 DEXs, and nearly 5.5 million tokens, a staggering rate of new token creation. He predicts that if current trends continue, the market could see approximately 1 billion new tokens in the next five years. This proliferation stems from the widespread availability of low-barrier tools, such as the pump.fun platform on Solana, which allows users to create tokens in seconds with permissionless and extremely low costs. This astronomical figure confirms that this mechanism significantly dilutes the liquidity of any meme: limited speculative funds are constantly attracted by new projects, preventing them from concentrating on a single asset and creating prosperity. On-chain data further confirms this dilution effect.
